item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP was just an excuse to make giant corgis roaming around SCP sites carrying people and materials canon, and you know what? I'm perfectly okay with that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Weird enough for an upvote, and the thought of a foundation transport by a bunch of giant corgis sent my sides to orbit. +1

item.title: Re: Editing needed
item.content:encoded:
It never really specifies how big the stick gets. It could be argued that the stick became larger but not to a point where it became unthrowable by a particularly strong person. You're right about it taking allot of throws to get back to a manageable size though, it's tedious but they could still manage it.

Imagine though,
Agent Stewart attempting to return SCP-3097 to its original size:
http://www.highlandgames.com/wp-content/uploads/2012/05/caber-toss-for-web-site-pic.jpg

item.title: Re: Editing needed
item.content:encoded: Yeah, but how do you throw a stick the size of a tree trunk back towards yourself? And even if you could, how many throws would you need to get it back to its original size?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for the fact that foundation goods are now transported on giant magic dogs.

item.title: Re: Editing needed
item.content:encoded:
the size of SCP-3097 will also decrease when thrown by someone towards themselves.

This would imply how they revert it back to a manageable size.

Also thankyou for the advice and look over

item.title: Re: Editing needed
item.content:encoded:
Grammar:
A person would propel a stick by throwing it. The stick does not propel. (If you mean it's spinning like a propeller, propellers are called that because they move the plane.)
"Instances of dog…" I'm not sure how to fix tis, but it's wrong.

Overall… it's a pretty simple and amusing concept, although it's missing some important information. For example, how do they get it back to the original size? Does it revert on landing? Important info. (Side thought, if it does, what happens if a dog catches it on the way down?)

I'm downvoting partially due to those issues, but also because I just can't buy the stuff in the second addendum. I think it's a very amusing plot twist, but I just can't suspend my disbelief so far, especially in service of such a short, plain narrative.

item.title: Editing needed
item.content:encoded: Iv'e checked over the article and corrected any grammatical errors iv'e spotted so far, but if anyone wishes to point any iv'e missed out or fix them themselves, that would be much appreciated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sufficiently weird enough for an upvote on its own, and if I hadn't already, that final note would've won me over regardless, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Quirky, simple, and doesn't overstay its welcome, though ultimately too problematic in the areas mentioned above for me to feel good about upvoting. No vote for now.

Reading it over once more, I can't really not upvote something so perfectly wacky. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll fixed what iv'e noticed so far, but if there's any sites or people you'd recommend who are willing to check it over that would be much appreciated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The execution, as Anborough says, needs some work. However, as the work largely lies in SPAG-related issues, it's rather easily fixed.

+1 in the hope that you'll make the necessary edits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel this is good, but the grammar leaves something to be desired.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm giving this a tentative upvote because I really like the concept, but it needs a thorough grammar and format scrub.
